title: AP World History: Modern DBQ Practice Exam 10

questions.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which resource nationalism changed global energy politics and economic relationships from c. 1950 to 1980.
Document 1
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Speech by a Venezuelan petroleum minister to a national economic council, 1959, Caracas.
Source Type: Government speech.
“For too long, oil beneath our soil has enriched foreign shareholders more reliably than Venezuelan schools, roads, and workers. We do not reject foreign expertise or markets, but concessions must serve the nation. A country that exports oil yet imports poverty has not achieved true sovereignty.”
Sourcing cues: Government minister from an oil-exporting state; links petroleum policy to sovereignty, development, and renegotiation of foreign concessions.
Document 2
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Statement from representatives of several oil-exporting governments after a meeting in Baghdad, 1960.
Source Type: Organization statement.
“Producer countries must consult one another when companies reduce posted prices or alter terms affecting national revenue. Petroleum is not merely a commodity; it is the principal income of many states. Cooperation among producers will allow governments to defend fair prices, stable revenue, and control over natural resources.”
Sourcing cues: Collective producer-state perspective; emphasizes cooperation against multinational oil company power.
Document 3
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Private letter from an executive of a multinational oil company to regional managers, 1968, London.
Source Type: Business letter.
“Several host governments now demand larger shares of revenue, local hiring, and participation in management. We must adapt where necessary, but excessive concessions risk encouraging similar demands elsewhere. Our advantage remains technical knowledge, refining capacity, shipping networks, and access to consumer markets.”
Sourcing cues: Corporate executive perspective; concerned about preserving company leverage while responding to resource nationalism.
Document 4
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Memorandum from a Nigerian petroleum official to the federal cabinet, 1972, Lagos.
Source Type: Government memorandum.
“Oil income offers the possibility of roads, universities, hospitals, and industrial projects, but it also increases regional competition over revenue. Foreign firms still control much technical knowledge, and rapid spending may import inflation rather than development. National participation in petroleum must be paired with training, planning, and accountability.”
Sourcing cues: Postcolonial state official; recognizes both opportunities and risks of oil revenue.
Document 5
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Energy price summary compiled by an international economic research office, 1960–1979.
Source Type: Plain-text data table.
Selected Oil Market Indicators, 1960–1979
Year Approximate Pattern in World Oil Markets
1960 Producer states begin coordinating over posted prices and revenue
1971 Several producer governments renegotiate company contracts
1973 Oil embargo and production decisions contribute to sharp price increases
1974 Importing countries face inflation, fuel shortages, and balance-of-payments pressure
1979 Political instability and supply concerns contribute to another major price shock
Note on the table: The report stated that oil-importing countries became more aware of their dependence on producer-state decisions.
Sourcing cues: International economic perspective; summarizes market shifts but does not show local social effects.
Document 6
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Description of a newspaper photograph, 1974, Tokyo.
Source Type: Described photograph.
The photograph shows office workers leaving a building with many lights turned off. A sign near the entrance reads “Save Energy for the Nation.” Nearby, commuters wait beside a crowded train platform while a newspaper headline announces higher fuel prices and reduced oil imports. The caption states: “The oil crisis reaches daily life.”
Sourcing cues: Urban consumer and importer-state perspective; shows how global energy politics affected ordinary routines in an industrial economy.
Document 7
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Statement by an Algerian trade union representative at a conference on national development, 1976, Algiers.
Source Type: Labor organization statement.
“National control over oil and gas is a victory only if workers and villages share in it. A flag over the refinery does not automatically bring housing, wages, or clean water. We support independence from foreign companies, but we also ask who controls the new state companies and whose labor builds the pipelines.”
Sourcing cues: Labor perspective within a postcolonial resource-exporting state; supports nationalization while questioning distribution and worker power.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 7
Thesis / Claim — 1 point: Earns the point by making a historically defensible claim that evaluates the extent to which resource nationalism changed global energy politics and economic relationships from c. 1950 to 1980. A strong thesis should establish a line of reasoning, such as arguing that producer states gained leverage over multinational corporations and importing countries, but that technology, markets, internal inequality, and dependence on oil revenue limited the transformation.
Contextualization — 1 point: Earns the point by describing a broader historical context relevant to resource nationalism, such as decolonization, postwar economic growth, multinational corporations, Cold War development, OPEC, postcolonial state-building, industrial dependence on petroleum, the Arab-Israeli conflict, or the rise of global commodity markets.
Evidence from the Documents — 2 points: Earns 1 point by accurately using the content of at least three documents to address the prompt. Earns 2 points by using at least six documents to support an argument about producer-state cooperation, renegotiated concessions, multinational company power, development planning, price shocks, importing-country vulnerability, or labor critiques of nationalization.
Evidence Beyond the Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by using at least one specific piece of relevant outside evidence not found in the documents, such as OPEC, the 1973 oil embargo, nationalization of oil in Mexico or Iran, the Seven Sisters oil companies, the Yom Kippur War, petrodollars, stagflation, North Sea oil, or import-substitution development strategies funded by oil revenue.
Sourcing / Analysis of Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by explaining how or why the point of view, purpose, audience, or historical situation of at least three documents is relevant to the argument. Effective examples include explaining why oil ministers framed petroleum as sovereignty, why producer-state statements emphasized collective action, why corporate executives worried about precedent, why importer photographs emphasized daily disruption, or why labor representatives supported national control while criticizing unequal distribution.
Complexity — 1 point: Earns the point by demonstrating a more complex understanding of resource nationalism. This may include explaining that producer states increased bargaining power without fully escaping global market dependence, that nationalization could strengthen states while not guaranteeing social equality, that importing countries remained powerful but vulnerable, or that oil politics connected postcolonial sovereignty, corporate power, labor, inflation, and Cold War development.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong DBQ could argue that resource nationalism significantly changed global energy politics by increasing the leverage of oil-producing states, encouraging producer cooperation, weakening older concession systems, and exposing importing countries to price shocks. Documents 1, 2, 4, and 7 support arguments about sovereignty, development, and national control. Document 3 shows multinational companies adapting while retaining technical and market power. Documents 5 and 6 show global price shocks and effects on importing societies. Outside evidence could include OPEC, the 1973 embargo, the Seven Sisters, the Yom Kippur War, petrodollars, stagflation, or oil nationalization in Mexico or Iran. Strong sourcing should compare producer governments, corporations, postcolonial officials, international analysts, consumers, and labor representatives.
